title: Why the body resists relaxation even when the mind wants it
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You've been telling yourself to relax for years

I know the feeling. You're lying in bed at 11pm and your jaw is clenched. Your shoulders are somewhere up near your ears. Your mind is running through every mistake you made in 2017. And you're thinking, just relax, why can't you just relax?

But your body doesn't listen. It won't. It can't.

Here's the thing. The body isn't being stubborn. It's not defective. It's not broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you alive.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system actually works. His research shows that the body's safety response isn't something we can just think our way into. It's a physiological state that has to be built, brick by brick, through experience and repetition.

And if your body learned, somewhere along the way, that relaxation was dangerous, it's going to resist. Hard.

I remember the first time a therapist told me to do a body scan. I lay on the floor and within thirty seconds my heart was pounding. My chest felt tight. I wanted to run. She said, "just notice that," and I wanted to throw something at her. Because noticing didn't help. It made everything worse.

That's the part nobody talks about. The wellness industry sells us this image of relaxation as this gentle, effortless drifting into peace. But for people with trauma histories, relaxation can feel like stepping into a trap.

Your body doesn't know the difference between letting your guard down on purpose and being ambushed. It just knows that when you relax, bad things have happened before.

The nervous system doesn't care about your intentions

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body. Not in your thoughts. Not in your memories. In your tissues, your fascia, your autonomic nervous system. The parts of you that don't speak English.

When you tell yourself to relax, you're using your prefrontal cortex. The thinking brain. The part that makes plans and sets goals. But the nervous system runs on older, deeper programming. It's scanning for threats at a speed your conscious mind can't even perceive.

And if it detects anything that even resembles a past threat, it's going to hit the brakes. Or the gas. Or both at once, which is what that wired-but-tired feeling actually is.

Think about it. If you grew up in a home where being quiet and still meant you were safe, your body might relax easily. But if you grew up in a home where being quiet and still meant you were an easier target, your body learned something different. It learned that vigilance equals survival.

Relaxation, in that context, is literally dangerous. Your nervous system isn't going to let you do something that got you hurt before, even if the threat is long gone.

I have a friend who can't do yoga. Every time she tries, she starts crying. Not sad crying. Full body sobbing that comes out of nowhere. Her well-meaning yoga teacher tells her to breathe through it. But her body is saying something real. It's saying, I need to move, I need to be ready, I can't lie here with my eyes closed.

That's not resistance in the way we usually mean it. That's wisdom. It's just ancient, wordless wisdom that doesn't know how to explain itself.

So when you sit down to meditate or take a bath or do a breathing exercise and your body goes into full revolt, don't add shame on top of it. Don't tell yourself you're doing it wrong. Your body has its reasons. They might not make sense to your thinking brain, but they make sense to your survival brain.

Stay with me here, because this is where it gets interesting. The way out isn't to force relaxation. The way out is to make the body feel safe enough to relax on its own.

What safety actually requires

Deb Dana, who adapted Polyvagal Theory for clinical practice, talks about something called "glimmers." These are micro-moments of safety that the nervous system can anchor to. A soft sound. A warm cup of tea. The feeling of a blanket. A kind voice.

But here's the hard part. If your nervous system has been in survival mode for years, it might not even register these glimmers. They're too subtle. Your system is calibrated for threat detection, not safety detection. You can be surrounded by safe things and still feel like you're in danger.

That's not a character flaw. That's a nervous system that learned a very specific pattern and got really good at it.

One of the things that helped me was understanding that my body's resistance wasn't personal. It wasn't a message about my worth or my progress or my spiritual development. It was just biology. A survival response that had outlived its usefulness but didn't know it yet.

I started small. Really small. Not deep breathing or meditation. Just sitting in a chair and noticing that my feet were on the floor. That's it. No trying to change anything. No trying to relax. Just noticing.

And sometimes my body would tense up more. And I'd just say, okay, I see that. You're scared. That makes sense.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why my body learned that being seen was dangerous. Why relaxation felt like exposure. Why safety was so hard to trust.

Slowly, over months, my body started to believe me. Not because I convinced it with logic, but because I showed it, over and over, that I wasn't going to force it. That I could handle its fear. That I wasn't going to abandon it when it got scared.

That's the work. Not forcing relaxation. Building enough trust that the body can risk it.

The trap of toxic relaxation culture

I need to be direct about something. The wellness industry has done real damage here. It's sold us this idea that if we just try hard enough, we can relax our way out of trauma. That if we can't calm down, it's because we're not trying the right thing, or we're not spiritual enough, or we're holding on too tight.

That's bullshit. And it's harmful bullshit.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, has shown that trauma actually changes the structure and function of the brain. The amygdala gets bigger and more reactive. The medial prefrontal cortex, which helps regulate emotion, gets smaller and less active. The hippocampus, which processes memory, can shrink.

These are physical changes. You can't meditate your way out of a brain that's been rewired for survival. That's not how neurobiology works.

What you can do is slowly, patiently, give the brain new experiences that teach it something different. But that takes time. It takes repetition. And it takes a lot of grace for the parts of you that can't just let go.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Because part of making the body feel safe is learning to say no to things that activate it. Boundaries aren't just for other people. They're for your own nervous system.

I stopped doing things that made my body feel worse in the name of relaxation. I stopped forcing myself to do breathing exercises that made me panic. I stopped going to yoga classes where the teacher talked about "releasing" things I didn't even know I was holding. I started listening to what my body actually needed, which was sometimes movement, sometimes stillness, sometimes just sitting in my car for ten minutes after work before going inside.

That's not glamorous. That doesn't sell courses or Instagram posts. But it's real.

What to do when your body says no

So what do you actually do when you want to relax but your body won't cooperate?

First, stop trying to relax. Seriously. The goal isn't relaxation. The goal is safety. And you can't achieve safety by fighting your own nervous system.

Second, get curious instead of judgmental. When your body tenses up, don't say, "why am I so broken?" Say, "oh, interesting, what's happening right now?" That shift from judgment to curiosity changes your physiology. It sends a signal to your nervous system that you're not in danger, you're just observing.

Third, pendulate. That's a term from Somatic Experiencing. It means moving between a place of activation and a place of relative calm. Not trying to get to total relaxation, just finding a slightly less activated state. If you're at a 9 on the panic scale, can you get to a 7? If you're at a 7, can you get to a 5? That's progress. That's real progress.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that help you track these states and work with them at your own pace.

Fourth, orient. Look around the room. Find five things you can see. Three things you can hear. One thing you can feel that's neutral, like the floor under your feet or the air on your skin. This is a Polyvagal technique that helps the nervous system realize, in real time, that the threat isn't here right now.

Fifth, and this is the hardest one, let your body have its reaction. If you need to shake, shake. If you need to cry, cry. If you need to pace, pace. Don't try to calm the reaction down. Let it move through. That's how the nervous system actually completes its stress response. By moving, not by suppressing.

I know this is hard. I know it feels like you should be further along. I know you've been trying for years and your body still doesn't trust safety.

But here's what I've learned. The body's resistance isn't proof that you're broken. It's proof that you survived. It's proof that your nervous system did exactly what it was supposed to do, for years, to keep you alive in conditions that should have broken you.

And that same nervous system can learn something new. It just needs time. And patience. And someone who won't give up on it.

That someone is you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
